---
title: "Chapter 32 — 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 31 · 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment](ch31.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [33 · 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use →](ch33.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is the technique of bolting an external search system onto a language model so that, instead of answering from frozen parametric memory alone, the model first *retrieves* relevant documents and then *generates* an answer grounded in them. It is the dominant pattern for building question-answering and assistant systems over private, current, or domain-specific knowledge, and it sits at the junction of information retrieval and generative LLMs.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Language, Vision & Modalities (applied LLM systems) · used to ground generation in an external, current, private knowledge source · the one key idea: *retrieve relevant text, then condition generation on it — search first, answer second.*
:::

## 32.1 — Why RAG exists

A pretrained LLM is a snapshot. Its weights encode whatever was in the training corpus up to a **knowledge cutoff**, and nothing after. Ask it about yesterday's outage, your company's internal runbook, or a contract it has never seen, and it has three bad options: refuse, guess, or **hallucinate** — produce fluent text that is confidently wrong because the model is optimised to be plausible, not truthful.

Retraining or fine-tuning the model on every new document is slow and expensive, and it still bakes knowledge into opaque weights you cannot easily update or cite. RAG sidesteps all of this. The knowledge lives *outside* the model in a searchable store. At question time you fetch the few passages that matter and paste them into the prompt as context. The model's job shrinks from "know everything" to "read these passages and answer."

Think of the difference between an exam taken from memory and an open-book exam. Fine-tuning is cramming facts into your head the night before; RAG is walking in with the textbook and a good index, so you only need to *find* the right page and *reason* about it. The second skill scales far better when the material keeps changing.

This buys four concrete things:

- **Currency** — update the store, not the weights. A new document indexed at 9:00 is answerable at 9:01.
- **Private data** — your wiki, tickets, and PDFs never need to enter anyone's training set.
- **Reduced hallucination** — grounding the answer in retrieved text constrains the model toward what the sources actually say.
- **Citations** — because you know which passages you fed in, you can show the user *where* each claim came from, making answers auditable.

::: {.callout-tip}
RAG and fine-tuning solve different problems. Fine-tuning teaches *behaviour and style* (how to answer); RAG supplies *knowledge* (what to answer with). When someone says "the model doesn't know our docs," the fix is almost always retrieval, not training.
:::

## 32.2 — Embeddings and semantic search

Keyword search fails when the user and the document use different words for the same thing — a query for "car" misses a page about "automobiles." The fix is to search by *meaning* rather than by surface tokens, and the tool for that is the **embedding**: a function that maps a piece of text to a vector $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (typically $d$ from 384 to 3072) such that texts with similar meaning land near each other in the space.

These embedding models (covered mechanically in the NLP and Transformers chapters) are trained so that paraphrases cluster together. Similarity is almost always measured with **cosine similarity** — the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which ignores their length and looks only at direction:

$$\text{sim}(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \frac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{\lVert \mathbf{a} \rVert \, \lVert \mathbf{b} \rVert}$$

A score of 1 means identical direction (same meaning), 0 means unrelated, −1 means opposite. The reason we ignore length is that a long document and a short query about the same topic point the same way even though one vector is "bigger" — direction carries the meaning, magnitude mostly carries length. **Semantic search** is then simple in principle: embed every document chunk once and store the vectors; at query time, embed the query and return the chunks whose vectors are closest.

Picture the embedding space as a map where "automobile," "car," and "sedan" sit in the same neighbourhood, while "banana" is on the far side of town. A query lands at a point, and search just looks for the nearest landmarks.

```python
import numpy as np

def cosine(a, b):
    # dot product over the product of norms
    return a @ b / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))

def search(query_vec, doc_vecs, k=3):
    # brute force: score every doc, take top-k
    scores = [cosine(query_vec, d) for d in doc_vecs]
    return np.argsort(scores)[::-1][:k]   # indices, best first
```

The brute-force scan above is *exact* — it compares the query against every vector. That is fine for thousands of chunks but collapses at millions, which is where approximate indexes (next section) come in.

::: {.callout-warning}
Embeddings are not interchangeable. A vector from one model is meaningless to another, and query and document must be embedded by the *same* model (or a matched pair). Re-embed your whole corpus when you switch models — silently mixing embedding spaces returns garbage neighbours.
:::

## 32.3 — Vector databases and approximate nearest neighbours

Once you have millions of vectors, scoring all of them per query is too slow. A **vector database** (FAISS, Pinecone, Milvus, pgvector, Qdrant, Weaviate) stores the vectors and builds an **approximate nearest neighbour (ANN)** index that finds *almost* the closest vectors in sub-linear time, trading a sliver of accuracy (measured as **recall** — the fraction of true neighbours the index actually returns) for enormous speed.

The intuition for why approximation is acceptable: in RAG you do not need the mathematically perfect top-10 — you need ten *good* passages, and a reranker (section 32.5) cleans up the order afterwards. Missing the 9th-best neighbour but finding the 11th-best instead costs almost nothing in answer quality while buying orders of magnitude in speed.

Two index families dominate:

- **IVF (Inverted File)** — cluster all vectors into buckets (via k-means) once at build time. At query time, only probe the few buckets nearest the query instead of the whole set. The `nprobe` knob trades recall for speed: probe more buckets, find more true neighbours, pay more time.
- **HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World)** — build a layered graph where each vector links to its near neighbours. Search starts at a sparse top layer for a coarse jump across the space, then descends into denser layers to refine, greedily walking toward the query. HNSW gives excellent recall at low latency and is the default in most modern stores; the cost is higher memory and slower inserts.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Q[query vector] --> E{index type}
  E -->|exact| B[scan all N vectors] --> R[top-k]
  E -->|IVF| C[probe nearest clusters] --> R
  E -->|HNSW| G[greedy walk down graph layers] --> R
```

The geometric intuition for HNSW: zoom out to take long hops, then zoom in to refine — like finding a house by flying to the right city, then driving to the right street, then walking to the door.

<svg viewBox="0 0 320 120" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <text x="8" y="14" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">layer 2 (sparse)</text>
  <circle cx="40" cy="30" r="4" fill="currentColor"/><circle cx="150" cy="30" r="4" fill="currentColor"/><circle cx="260" cy="30" r="4" fill="currentColor"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="30" x2="150" y2="30" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="150" y1="30" x2="260" y2="30" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="8" y="74" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">layer 0 (dense)</text>
  <circle cx="40" cy="90" r="3" fill="currentColor"/><circle cx="95" cy="90" r="3" fill="currentColor"/><circle cx="150" cy="90" r="3" fill="currentColor"/><circle cx="205" cy="90" r="3" fill="currentColor"/><circle cx="260" cy="90" r="3" fill="currentColor"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="90" x2="95" y2="90" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="95" y1="90" x2="150" y2="90" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="150" y1="90" x2="205" y2="90" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="205" y1="90" x2="260" y2="90" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="150" y1="34" x2="150" y2="86" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3 3"/>
  <text x="120" y="108" font-size="8" fill="currentColor">descend to refine</text>
</svg>

| Index | Build cost | Query speed | Recall | Memory | Best for |
|-------|-----------|-------------|--------|--------|----------|
| Exact (flat) | none | slow (O(N)) | 100% | low | < ~10⁵ vectors, ground truth |
| IVF | moderate | fast | tunable (`nprobe`) | moderate | large static corpora |
| HNSW | high | very fast | high | high | low-latency, frequent reads |

A practical note on storage cost: a million 1536-dimensional float32 vectors is about 6 GB of raw vectors alone, and HNSW adds the graph on top. This is why production systems often apply **quantization** — storing each dimension as an 8-bit integer or a compressed code instead of a 32-bit float — to shrink memory four-fold or more, at a small recall cost. It is the same speed-vs-accuracy bargain, applied to memory.

## 32.4 — Chunking strategies

You cannot embed a 50-page PDF as one vector — meaning would be averaged into mush, and you would retrieve the whole document when the user needs one paragraph. So documents are split into **chunks**: passages small enough to be a focused unit of retrieval, large enough to carry self-contained meaning.

Chunking is the most underrated lever in RAG. Chunk too small and a passage loses the context that makes it answerable ("it increased by 12%" — what did?). Chunk too large and the embedding blurs multiple topics together and you waste context-window budget feeding the model irrelevant sentences. Common strategies, roughly in order of sophistication:

- **Fixed-size** — split every $N$ tokens (say 256–512). Simple, but blindly cuts mid-sentence.
- **Overlapping windows** — fixed-size chunks that share a sliding overlap (e.g. 50 tokens) so a fact spanning a boundary survives intact in at least one chunk.
- **Recursive / structural** — split on natural boundaries first (paragraphs, then sentences), respecting the document's structure so chunks align with ideas.
- **Semantic** — start a new chunk when the topic shifts, detected by a drop in embedding similarity between adjacent sentences.

A practical default is recursive splitting at ~300–500 tokens with ~10–15% overlap. Always store **metadata** with each chunk — source URI, title, section, timestamp — both to power citations and to allow filtered retrieval ("only docs from this team, after this date").

A useful refinement worth knowing is decoupling *what you embed* from *what you return*. You can embed a small, sharp unit (a single sentence, or an LLM-generated summary of a section) for precise matching, but then hand the model the larger surrounding passage once that unit is retrieved. This "small-to-big" trick gets the precision of fine chunks with the context of coarse ones.

::: {.callout-warning}
The chunk you retrieve is the chunk the model reads. If a chunk omits the heading or the table caption that gives a number its meaning, retrieval can be perfect and the answer still wrong. Test your chunking by eyeballing the actual retrieved chunks, not just the final answers.
:::

## 32.5 — The RAG pipeline, hybrid search, and reranking

A production RAG system is a pipeline with an offline (indexing) half and an online (query) half. Documents are ingested, chunked, embedded, and stored once; queries then flow through retrieve → rerank → generate on every request.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  subgraph Offline [Indexing - once]
    D[documents] --> C[chunk] --> EM[embed chunks] --> VS[(vector store)]
  end
  subgraph Online [Query time - every request]
    Q[user query] --> EQ[embed query]
    EQ --> RET[retrieve top-k candidates]
    VS --> RET
    RET --> RR[rerank with cross-encoder]
    RR --> P[build prompt with top passages]
    P --> LLM[generate grounded answer + citations]
  end
```

Two refinements separate a toy from a system.

**Hybrid search.** Dense embedding search is great at meaning but weak at exact tokens — product codes, error numbers, rare names, where the *literal string* matters. Classic **BM25** keyword retrieval (a TF-IDF-style sparse scorer) is the opposite: it nails exact terms but is blind to synonyms. Run both and fuse their rankings — commonly with **Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)**, which scores each document by summing $\frac{1}{k + r_i}$ over its rank $r_i$ in each list:

$$\text{RRF}(d) = \sum_{i} \frac{1}{k + r_i(d)}$$

The beauty of RRF is that it fuses on *rank position* rather than raw scores, so you never have to reconcile a cosine similarity with a BM25 score that live on completely different scales. The result gets the best of both worlds: semantic recall *and* exact-match precision. Hybrid search is one of the highest-ROI upgrades in RAG.

**Cross-encoder reranking.** The initial retrieval uses a **bi-encoder**: query and documents are embedded *separately*, so it is fast (documents are pre-embedded) but the query never "sees" the document during scoring. A **cross-encoder** instead feeds the query and one candidate passage *together* through a transformer and outputs a single relevance score — far more accurate, because every query word can attend to every document word, but far too slow to run over the whole corpus. The standard pattern uses each where it shines: retrieve ~50–100 candidates cheaply with the bi-encoder, then rerank just those with the cross-encoder and keep the top 3–5.

| Stage | Model type | Scope | Speed | Accuracy |
|-------|-----------|-------|-------|----------|
| Retrieve | bi-encoder (ANN) | whole corpus | very fast | coarse |
| Rerank | cross-encoder | ~100 candidates | slow per pair | precise |

This two-stage "retrieve cheap, rerank expensive" shape recurs all over machine learning systems: use a fast, approximate filter to cut the candidate set down, then spend an expensive, accurate model only on the survivors.

## 32.6 — Lost in the middle, and how context order matters

You might think: if retrieval is good, just stuff 30 chunks into the giant context window and let the model sort it out. It does not work that way. LLMs exhibit a **"lost in the middle"** effect — they attend most reliably to information at the *beginning* and the *end* of the context, and accuracy sags for relevant facts buried in the *middle* of a long prompt. Plot accuracy against the position of the needed fact and the curve is U-shaped: high at the edges, low in the belly.

This has direct design consequences. More retrieved chunks is not strictly better: padding the prompt with marginally relevant passages can *bury* the one that matters and dilute the model's attention. The implications:

- **Retrieve fewer, better passages** — precision beats recall once you have enough; this is exactly what reranking buys you.
- **Order by relevance, and place the strongest passages at the edges** — some pipelines deliberately put the top hit first and the second-best last, folding the ranked list around the U.
- **Keep the prompt tight** — every irrelevant token competes for the model's limited attention budget.

<svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <line x1="30" y1="95" x2="285" y2="95" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="95" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="2" y="20" font-size="8" fill="currentColor">acc</text>
  <text x="34" y="108" font-size="8" fill="currentColor">start</text>
  <text x="140" y="108" font-size="8" fill="currentColor">middle</text>
  <text x="255" y="108" font-size="8" fill="currentColor">end</text>
  <path d="M35 25 Q 90 30 110 55 T 160 70 Q 200 60 230 35 T 280 25" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="120" y="50" font-size="8" fill="currentColor">lost here</text>
</svg>

::: {.callout-tip}
If adding more retrieved context makes answers *worse*, you are probably hitting lost-in-the-middle. The fix is rarely a bigger model — it is reranking and trimming to fewer, sharper chunks.
:::

## 32.7 — Evaluating RAG

A RAG system can fail at two independent stages, and you must measure them separately. **Retrieval** can fail to surface the right passage; **generation** can fail to use a passage that *was* retrieved. A single end-to-end "is the answer good?" score hides which half is broken — and you cannot fix what you cannot localize.

The retrieval half is scored with classic information-retrieval metrics: **recall@k** (did the right chunk make the top $k$?), **precision@k** (what fraction of the returned chunks were actually relevant?), and rank-aware measures like **MRR** (mean reciprocal rank, which rewards putting the right chunk near the top) and **nDCG**. If recall@k is low, no amount of prompt engineering saves you — the right text never reached the model, so fix chunking, embeddings, or hybrid search first.

The generation half is scored on three axes now standard in RAG evaluation (e.g. the RAGAS framework):

- **Faithfulness** — is every claim in the answer *supported by the retrieved context*? This is the direct measure of hallucination: an unsupported claim is a faithfulness failure even if it happens to be true.
- **Answer relevance** — does the answer actually address the question that was asked?
- **Context relevance / precision** — were the retrieved passages on-topic, or padded with noise?

A common and scalable way to grade faithfulness and relevance is **LLM-as-judge**: prompt a capable model to check, claim by claim, whether the answer is entailed by the context. It is far cheaper than human annotation and correlates reasonably well, though it inherits the judge model's biases and should be spot-checked against human labels rather than trusted blindly.

::: {.callout-warning}
High faithfulness alone is not success — a system that answers "I don't know" to everything is perfectly faithful and completely useless. Track faithfulness *and* answer relevance together; they trade off against each other, and you want both high at once.
:::

## 32.8 — Agentic RAG

Classic RAG is a straight line: one query in, one retrieval, one answer. That breaks on hard questions — multi-hop queries ("compare our 2023 and 2024 security incidents") that need several retrievals, ambiguous queries that need rewriting before they will match anything, or questions where the first retrieval comes back empty and the system should *try again differently* rather than hallucinate.

**Agentic RAG** wraps retrieval in a reasoning loop driven by the LLM itself (see the AI Agents chapter for the general pattern). Instead of always retrieving once, the model decides *whether* to retrieve, *what* to search for, and *whether the results are good enough* — looping until it can answer. Typical added moves:

- **Query rewriting / decomposition** — turn a messy or compound question into one or more clean search queries.
- **Retrieve, then critique** — judge whether the returned chunks are sufficient; if not, reformulate and search again.
- **Routing** — pick the right source (which index, the web, a SQL database, a tool) per sub-question.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  Q[user query] --> A[agent: plan / rewrite]
  A --> R[retrieve]
  R --> J{enough to answer?}
  J -->|no| A
  J -->|yes| G[generate grounded answer]
```

The tradeoff is the usual one for agents: more retrievals and LLM calls mean higher latency and cost, and a loop that can run several times needs a step cap and a fallback so that it always terminates instead of spinning. Reach for agentic RAG when questions genuinely need multi-step reasoning over the corpus — not as a default for simple lookups, where plain retrieve-then-generate is faster, cheaper, and far easier to debug.

## 32.x — Key takeaways

- RAG grounds an LLM in an external store so it can answer from current, private knowledge with citations — fixing the knowledge-cutoff problem and curbing hallucination without retraining. It supplies *knowledge*; fine-tuning supplies *behaviour*.
- Semantic search relies on **embeddings** and cosine similarity; an exact scan works for small corpora, while **ANN indexes (HNSW, IVF)** trade a little recall for huge speed at scale, and quantization trades a little recall for much less memory.
- **Chunking** is a primary quality lever: split on structure, keep modest overlap, carry metadata for citations and filtering, and consider embedding small but returning big.
- The pipeline is *ingest → chunk → embed → store → retrieve → rerank → generate*; **hybrid search (BM25 + dense, fused with RRF)** and **cross-encoder reranking** are the highest-ROI upgrades.
- LLMs suffer **lost in the middle** — favour fewer, well-ranked passages over stuffing the context window full.
- Evaluate retrieval (recall@k, MRR) and generation (**faithfulness**, answer relevance) *separately*; **agentic RAG** adds query rewriting and retrieval loops for hard multi-hop questions, at the cost of latency and complexity.

## 32.y — See also

- **Chapter 28 — Natural Language Processing** — embeddings, BM25/TF-IDF, and the information-retrieval foundations RAG builds on.
- **Chapter 30 — Large Language Models** — the generators RAG conditions, knowledge cutoff, and context-window behaviour.
- **Chapter 31 — Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment** — the RAG-vs-fine-tuning choice and how to prompt effectively with retrieved context.
- **Chapter 33 — AI Agents & Tool Use** — the reasoning-loop and routing patterns behind agentic RAG.
- **Chapter 18 — Attention & Transformers** — the attention mechanism that explains both how embeddings are produced and why lost-in-the-middle happens.
- **Chapter 10 — Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning** — the high-dimensional vector geometry that vector search operates in.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 31 · 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment](ch31.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [33 · 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use →](ch33.html)
